perturbation.original_text: 4.6 The parties shall comply at all times with the Personal Data Protection Act 2012.
perturbation.changed_text:
21.8 In the event of a conflict between any of the ter ms of this Agreement, including its Schedules and Annexes, the conflict will be resolved in the following order of priority: (1) the Clauses of this Agreement; (2) the Schedules and Annexes.
4.6 The parties shall comply at all times with the Personal Data Protection Act 2012.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the data protection compliance clause to the very end makes it less visible and potentially overlooked. This is especially problematic because it is related to the entire agreement but included inside a sub-section related to conflict resolution between clauses, schedules and annexes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the clause to a less prominent and relevant position within the contract reduces its visibility and may be seen as a failure to adequately emphasize the importance of data protection as required by Singapore's PDPA. Section 4(1) of the PDPA establishes the baseline obligation for organizations to be accountable for personal data in their possession or control.

perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of burying the data protection clause within a conflict resolution section creates a legal contradiction. This arrangement reduces the visibility and emphasis on data protection, potentially conflicting with the baseline obligation for organizations to be accountable for personal data, as suggested by the law explanation. While neither snippet explicitly details this requirement, the explanation states that Section 4(1) of the PDPA establishes this accountability. By placing the clause in a less prominent position, the modified contract undermines the importance of compliance, supporting a 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: 9. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY In carrying out their respective obligations under this Agreement, the Company and AHPL shall comply with all laws and regulations applicable thereto but save for wilful acts, default or gross negligence on their respective parts, neither party shall be liable to the other party for any indirect, incidental, special, punitive or consequential damages however caused, including any loss of profit s or business interruption costs and under any theory of liability, including but not limited to contract, strict liability and negligence; whether or not the other party has been advised of the possibility of such damage.
perturbation.changed_text:
6. CONFIDENTIALITY OF THIS AGREEMENT
9. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY In carrying out their respective obligations under this Agreement, the Company and AHPL shall comply with all laws and regulations applicable thereto but save for wilful acts, default or gross negligence on their respective parts, neither party shall be liable to the other party for any indirect, incidental, special, punitive or consequential damages however caused, including any loss of profit s or business interruption costs and under any theory of liability, including but not limited to contract, strict liability and negligence; whether or not the other party has been advised of the possibility of such damage.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the limitation of liability clause under 'Confidentiality of this Agreement' obscures its importance and could lead a party to overlook it when assessing risk. Limitation of liability is a crucial aspect of contract law and warrants its own distinct section or a more relevant one.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 2(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore's Unfair Contract Terms Act regulates the exclusion or limitation of liability. Placing the liability limitation clause under the 'Confidentiality' section could make it less conspicuous and harder to review, thus potentially running afoul of the principles of fair contracting. Section 2(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act states that a person cannot exclude or restrict his liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and a
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a structural flaw by placing the 'Limitation of Liability' clause under the 'Confidentiality' section. This obscures its importance, making it difficult for parties to locate and interpret, which contradicts the principles of fair contracting. The Law Explanation indicates this may run afoul of fair contracting principles because it is less conspicuous. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states Singapore contract law is based on English common law, but has been modified by specific statutes. This suggests that clear placement of clauses is important for enforceability and awareness, and its placement under a misleading section could make it harder to review.
